title: README
content: IT Help Desk Knowledge Base Overview This knowledge base contains documentation used by the AI-powered IT Help Desk Chatbot. It includes: ITIL concepts ITSM processes Incident SOPs Service Request SOPs Troubleshooting Guides Frequently Asked Questions Company Policies The chatbot retrieves information from these documents to answer user questions accurately. Folder Structure itil/ incidents/ service_requests/ troubleshooting/ sop/ policies/ faq.md glossary.md Intended Users Employees Help Desk Engineers IT Administrators Interns Supported Topics Incident Management Service Requests Password Reset VPN Outlook Network Printer Laptop Windows Microsoft Teams Asset Management Version 1.0 v

title: Frequently Asked Questions
content: Frequently Asked Questions What is an Incident? An incident is an unexpected interruption or reduction in the quality of an IT service that requires restoration as quickly as possible. What is a Service Request? A Service Request is a request for a standard IT service such as software installation, VPN access, or a new laptop. What is the difference between an Incident and a Service Request? Incident Service Request Unexpected issue Planned request Service interruption Standard service Restore service Fulfill request What is ITIL? ITIL is a framework of best practices for managing IT services and improving service quality. What is ITSM? IT Service Management (ITSM) is the practice of designing, delivering, supporting, and improving IT services. What is an SLA? A Service Level Agreement (SLA) defines the expected response and resolution times for IT services. When should I contact the Service Desk? Contact the Service Desk when: You cannot access company systems. You need software installed. Your password has expired. Your laptop or desktop is not functioning correctly. You need access to company resources. How do I report an incident? Provide: Your name Device name Department Description of the issue Error message Screenshot (if available) How do I request a new laptop? Submit a Service Request with manager approval. The IT team will verify inventory, configure the device, and assign it to you. Keywords FAQ ITSM ITIL Incident Service Request Help Desk SLA

title: glossary
content: ITSM Glossary ITSM IT Service Management ITIL Information Technology Infrastructure Library SLA Service Level Agreement Incident An unplanned interruption to an IT service. Service Request A formal request for a standard IT service. Problem The root cause of one or more incidents. Change A planned modification to an IT service or infrastructure. Service Desk The single point of contact between users and IT support. Asset Any hardware or software owned by the organization. CMDB Configuration Management Database used to store information about IT assets and their relationships. CAB Change Advisory Board responsible for reviewing and approving significant changes. VPN Virtual Private Network used to securely access company resources remotely. Active Directory Microsoft directory service used for authentication and user management. Microsoft 365 Cloud productivity platform including Outlook, Teams, OneDrive, and Office applications.

title: Ticket Escalation SOP
content: Ticket Escalation SOP Purpose This SOP explains when and how incidents should be escalated to ensure timely resolution and compliance with Service Level Agreements (SLAs). Overview Escalation is the process of transferring an incident to a higher support level or another specialized team when it cannot be resolved by the current support engineer. Types of Escalation Functional Escalation Transfer the ticket to a team with specialized technical knowledge. Examples: Network Team Server Team Database Team Security Team Hierarchical Escalation Notify management when: SLA is at risk. Business impact increases. Major incident occurs. Escalation Criteria Escalate when: Issue cannot be resolved within the Service Desk. Administrator privileges are required. Hardware replacement is needed. Security incident is suspected. Multiple users are affected. SLA target is close to being breached. Escalation Workflow Document troubleshooting already completed. Update the incident ticket. Assign the ticket to the appropriate support team. Inform the user. Monitor ticket progress. Verify resolution before closure. Best Practices Never escalate without performing initial troubleshooting. Include screenshots and error messages. Record all actions taken. Keep the user informed throughout the process. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Service Desk Service Level Management Keywords Escalation Support SLA Major Incident Ticket Support Team

title: incident_overview
content: Incident Overview Purpose This document explains what an incident is, how incidents are handled, and the responsibilities of the IT Service Desk during the incident lifecycle. Definition An incident is an unplanned interruption to an IT service or a reduction in the quality of an IT service. The primary objective of Incident Management is to restore normal service as quickly as possible. Examples of Incidents Computer not starting Printer offline Outlook not opening VPN connection failure Network outage Blue Screen of Death (BSOD) Email not sending Shared drive inaccessible Incident Categories Hardware Laptop Desktop Printer Monitor Keyboard Mouse Software Microsoft Office Outlook Teams SAP Browser Network Wi-Fi VPN LAN Internet Security Account locked Malware Suspicious email Unauthorized access Incident Lifecycle Incident Detection Ticket Creation Categorization Priority Assignment Investigation Resolution User Confirmation Closure Information Required When logging an incident, collect: User name Employee ID (if applicable) Device name Location Contact details Description of the issue Error message Time of occurrence Screenshots (if available) Responsibilities User Reports the issue accurately. Service Desk Logs, categorizes, prioritizes, and resolves incidents whenever possible. Technical Support Team Handles complex technical issues. Ticket Closure A ticket should only be closed after: The issue has been resolved. The user confirms the resolution. Resolution details are documented. Best Practices Log every incident. Gather complete information. Communicate clearly with users. Update tickets regularly. Follow SLA timelines. Related ITIL Practices Service Desk Problem Management Knowledge Management Keywords Incident Ticket Support Resolution SLA Help Desk ITSM Service Desk

title: incident_priority
content: Incident Priority Matrix Purpose This document defines how incident priority is determined based on the impact and urgency of an issue. Proper prioritization ensures that critical business services are restored as quickly as possible. Overview Priority is assigned using two factors: Impact – How many users or business services are affected. Urgency – How quickly the issue must be resolved. Higher impact and urgency result in a higher priority. Priority Levels P1 – Critical Description A critical incident affecting business operations. Examples Entire network is down. Email server is unavailable. Production system outage. Company-wide VPN failure. Response Immediate attention. Notify Incident Manager. Escalate to senior support teams. Continuous communication until resolved. P2 – High Description A major issue affecting multiple users or an important department. Examples Department cannot access shared drives. Printer server failure. Teams not working for multiple users. Response High priority investigation. Escalate if unresolved within SLA. P3 – Medium Description An issue affecting a single user with an available workaround. Examples Outlook not opening. VPN issue for one employee. Printer offline for one user. Slow computer. Response Standard troubleshooting. Resolve within normal SLA. P4 – Low Description Minor issues or information requests. Examples Wallpaper change. General software guidance. Password change request. User training request. Response Resolve as resources become available. Priority Matrix Impact Urgency Priority High High P1 High Medium P2 Medium Medium P2 Medium Low P3 Low Medium P3 Low Low P4 Best Practices Assess business impact before assigning priority. Review priority if the situation changes. Escalate major incidents immediately. Keep users informed. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Service Level Management Keywords Priority P1 P2 P3 P4 Impact Urgency SLA

title: network_issue
content: Network Issue SOP Purpose Provide a standard troubleshooting procedure for resolving network connectivity issues. Common Symptoms No internet connection Slow internet Wi-Fi disconnected Cannot access shared drives Unable to reach company servers DNS errors Limited connectivity Initial Checks Verify: Network cable is connected. Wi-Fi is enabled. Router or switch appears operational. Other users are affected. Troubleshooting Procedure Step 1 Restart the computer. Step 2 Reconnect to the network. Step 3 Check IP configuration. Command: ipconfig Step 4 Renew IP address. Commands: ipconfig /release ipconfig /renew Step 5 Test network connectivity. Commands: ping google.com ping <gateway_ip> Step 6 Flush DNS cache. Command: ipconfig /flushdns Step 7 Restart the network adapter. Step 8 Verify access to required resources. Common Causes Network cable disconnected Router failure Switch failure DNS issue DHCP issue ISP outage Firewall restrictions Escalation Escalate if: Multiple users are affected. Switch or router failure is suspected. Server cannot be reached. WAN connection is unavailable. Best Practices Use approved network hardware. Maintain updated drivers. Report recurring connectivity issues. Document troubleshooting performed. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Monitoring and Event Management Keywords Network Internet Wi-Fi LAN DNS DHCP Router Switch Connectivity Ping

title: outlook_issue
content: Outlook Issue SOP Purpose This document provides a standard troubleshooting procedure for resolving Microsoft Outlook issues. Common Symptoms Outlook not opening Outlook freezes or crashes Emails not sending Emails not receiving Outlook running slowly Error while opening Outlook Profile corruption Initial Checks Before troubleshooting: Verify the computer has internet access. Check whether Microsoft 365 services are operational. Confirm Outlook is installed correctly. Ask the user when the problem started. Troubleshooting Procedure Step 1 Restart Microsoft Outlook. Step 2 Restart the computer. Step 3 Open Outlook in Safe Mode. Command: outlook.exe /safe If Outlook opens successfully, disable unnecessary add-ins. Step 4 Check mailbox storage. Delete unnecessary emails if the mailbox is full. Step 5 Repair Microsoft Office. Go to: Control Panel → Programs → Microsoft Office → Change → Quick Repair If required, perform an Online Repair. Step 6 Create a new Outlook profile if the existing profile is corrupted. Step 7 Verify Outlook can connect to the mail server. Step 8 Confirm with the user that Outlook is functioning normally. Common Causes Corrupted Outlook profile Faulty add-ins Network connectivity issue Mail server issue Damaged Office installation Full mailbox Escalation Escalate if: Mail server is unavailable. Multiple users report the same issue. Profile recreation fails. Office repair does not resolve the issue. Best Practices Keep Microsoft Office updated. Archive old emails regularly. Disable unused add-ins. Maintain mailbox storage below organizational limits. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Knowledge Management Keywords Outlook Email Office Mailbox Profile Add-ins Microsoft 365 Safe Mode

title: password_reset
content: Password Reset SOP Purpose Provide a secure and standardized process for resetting a user's password while maintaining account security. When to Use Use this procedure when: User forgot password. Password has expired. User account is locked. Temporary password is required. Prerequisites Before resetting the password: Verify the user's identity. Confirm employee ID or username. Verify department or manager if required. Ensure the request is authorized. Procedure Step 1 Receive the password reset request. Step 2 Verify the user's identity. Step 3 Open Active Directory Users and Computers (or the organization's identity management system). Step 4 Locate the user's account. Step 5 Select Reset Password . Step 6 Assign a temporary password following the organization's password policy. Step 7 Enable: User must change password at next login. Step 8 Provide the temporary password securely. Never send passwords through unsecured channels. Step 9 Ask the user to log in and confirm successful access. Validation Confirm that: User can log in successfully. Password change is completed. No account lock exists. Escalation Escalate if: User account cannot be located. Active Directory is unavailable. Multiple password reset attempts fail. Identity cannot be verified. Best Practices Never disclose passwords to unauthorized persons. Verify identity before every reset. Follow password policy. Document the activity in the ticket. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Information Security Management Access Management Keywords Password Reset Account Locked Login Active Directory Authentication

title: printer_issue
content: Printer Issue SOP Purpose Provide a structured troubleshooting process for resolving printer-related incidents. Common Symptoms Printer offline Paper jam Print queue stuck Unable to print Slow printing Poor print quality Printer not detected Driver error Initial Checks Before troubleshooting: Verify printer power. Check cables. Ensure the printer is connected to the network. Confirm the correct printer is selected. Troubleshooting Procedure Step 1 Restart the printer. Step 2 Restart the user's computer. Step 3 Verify the printer status. If the printer shows Offline , reconnect it to the network. Step 4 Clear the print queue. Delete any stuck print jobs. Step 5 Restart the Print Spooler service. Step 6 Update or reinstall the printer driver if necessary. Step 7 Print a test page. Step 8 Confirm the issue is resolved with the user. Common Causes Network connectivity issue Incorrect printer selection Printer offline Driver corruption Print Spooler stopped Paper jam Low toner Hardware fault Escalation Escalate if: Printer hardware is damaged. Network connectivity cannot be restored. Driver installation repeatedly fails. Multiple users report the same issue. Best Practices Keep printer drivers updated. Use approved drivers. Remove stuck print jobs promptly. Perform regular printer maintenance. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Knowledge Management Keywords Printer Offline Print Queue Driver Paper Jam Print Spooler Network Printer

title: change_management
content: Change Management Purpose Change Management is the ITIL practice that ensures changes to IT services are planned, assessed, approved, implemented, and reviewed in a controlled manner. The objective is to minimize risks while improving IT services without causing unnecessary service disruptions. Overview A change is any addition, modification, or removal of an IT service, infrastructure component, software, hardware, or configuration item that could affect business operations. Examples of changes include: Installing a new server Updating Windows Upgrading Microsoft Office Deploying a new application Replacing network switches Updating firewall rules Unlike incidents, changes are planned activities. Objectives Reduce risks associated with changes. Minimize service downtime. Improve service quality. Ensure proper approvals before implementation. Maintain complete documentation of all changes. Types of Changes Standard Change A low-risk, pre-approved, and repeatable change. Examples: Installing approved software Resetting user permissions Creating user accounts Normal Change A planned change requiring review and approval. Examples: Server upgrade Network configuration change Database migration Emergency Change A change implemented immediately to restore critical services. Examples: Security vulnerability patch Critical server outage Firewall rule update during an attack Change Lifecycle Submit Change Request Review Change Risk Assessment Approval Implementation Planning Testing Deployment Validation Documentation Change Closure Roles and Responsibilities Requester Submits the change request. Change Manager Reviews and coordinates the change process. Change Advisory Board (CAB) Evaluates significant changes and provides approval. Technical Team Implements and validates the change. Risk Assessment Before implementing a change, consider: Business impact Technical complexity Rollback availability Security risks Service downtime Testing requirements Rollback Plan Every significant change should include a rollback plan. Example: If a software upgrade fails: Restore previous version. Restore backup. Verify service availability. Notify stakeholders. Best Practices Test changes before deployment. Schedule changes during maintenance windows. Inform affected users. Document implementation steps. Keep rollback plans ready. Review successful and failed changes. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Problem Management Release Management Deployment Management Keywords Change CAB Standard Change Normal Change Emergency Change Rollback Deployment Risk Assessment Maintenance

title: incident_management
content: Incident Management Purpose The purpose of Incident Management is to restore normal IT services as quickly as possible while minimizing the impact on business operations. Overview An incident is an unplanned interruption to an IT service or a reduction in the quality of an IT service. Examples include: Printer offline Outlook not opening VPN not connecting Computer not starting Wi-Fi disconnected Incident Management focuses on restoring service quickly rather than identifying the root cause. Incident Lifecycle Incident Identification Ticket Logging Categorization Prioritization Initial Diagnosis Investigation Resolution User Confirmation Ticket Closure Priority Levels P1 – Critical Business-wide outage Server down Network outage P2 – High Department affected Multiple users impacted P3 – Medium Single user issue Limited business impact P4 – Low Minor inconvenience Cosmetic issue General information request Roles and Responsibilities User Reports the issue. Service Desk Logs and investigates the incident. Support Team Resolves technical issues. Incident Manager Coordinates major incidents and communicates with stakeholders. Resolution Process Verify the issue. Collect relevant information. Search the knowledge base. Apply troubleshooting steps. Confirm the issue is resolved. Update the ticket. Close the incident. Escalation Escalate when: Resolution is beyond Service Desk capability. SLA is at risk. Major outage occurs. Vendor support is required. Best Practices Log every incident. Prioritize correctly. Keep users informed. Follow documented procedures. Meet SLA targets. Record the final resolution. Related ITIL Practices Service Desk Problem Management Change Enablement Keywords Incident Priority P1 P2 P3 P4 SLA Help Desk Support Resolution Ticket

title: knowledge_management
content: Knowledge Management Purpose Knowledge Management ensures that useful information is created, maintained, shared, and reused to improve IT support efficiency and service quality. Overview Knowledge Management helps support teams solve issues faster by providing documented solutions, procedures, FAQs, troubleshooting guides, and best practices. Instead of solving the same problem repeatedly, support engineers can use existing knowledge articles. Objectives Reduce resolution time. Improve first-contact resolution. Share knowledge across IT teams. Improve consistency in support. Reduce repeated work. Types of Knowledge Known Errors Documented problems with temporary workarounds. SOPs Standard Operating Procedures used to perform routine tasks. FAQs Frequently asked questions from users. Troubleshooting Guides Step-by-step procedures for resolving common issues. Technical Documentation Configuration details, architecture diagrams, and installation procedures. Knowledge Article Structure A good knowledge article should include: Title Purpose Symptoms Cause Resolution Steps Escalation Related Articles Keywords Benefits Faster ticket resolution Better user experience Reduced training time Consistent troubleshooting Improved collaboration Best Practices Keep articles updated. Use simple language. Include screenshots where applicable. Remove outdated information. Review articles regularly. Tag articles with keywords. Example Problem: VPN not connecting. Knowledge Article: Verify internet connection. Restart VPN client. Check credentials. Verify VPN server status. Contact Network Support if unresolved. Related ITIL Practices Service Desk Incident Management Problem Management Keywords Knowledge Knowledge Base Documentation FAQ SOP Troubleshooting Guide Known Error

title: problem_management
content: Problem Management Purpose Problem Management aims to identify and eliminate the root causes of recurring incidents to prevent them from happening again. Overview A problem is the underlying cause of one or more incidents. While Incident Management focuses on restoring service quickly, Problem Management focuses on finding permanent solutions. Example: Users repeatedly report that Outlook crashes every morning. Incident Management resolves each case temporarily. Problem Management investigates why Outlook crashes and implements a permanent fix. Objectives Prevent recurring incidents. Reduce downtime. Improve service quality. Identify root causes. Improve user satisfaction. Problem Lifecycle Problem Identification Problem Logging Investigation Root Cause Analysis Workaround Identification Permanent Solution Problem Closure Root Cause Analysis Techniques Common techniques include: Five Whys Fishbone Diagram Fault Tree Analysis Log Analysis System Monitoring Known Error A Known Error is a problem whose root cause has been identified and documented along with a workaround. Example: Problem: Printer driver crashes after Windows Update. Known Error: Install updated printer driver version 3.2. Workaround A workaround is a temporary solution that restores service until a permanent fix is available. Examples: Restart Outlook Restart Print Spooler Reconnect VPN Reboot the computer Roles Problem Manager Coordinates investigations and tracks problems. Technical Teams Perform root cause analysis. Service Desk Links recurring incidents to existing problems. Best Practices Investigate recurring incidents. Document workarounds. Maintain a Known Error Database (KEDB). Share knowledge with support teams. Monitor trends to identify recurring issues. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Knowledge Management Change Enablement Keywords Problem Root Cause Known Error KEDB Workaround Recurring Incident Problem Management ITIL

title: service_desk
content: Service Desk Purpose The Service Desk serves as the single point of contact (SPOC) between users and the IT department. It is responsible for handling incidents, fulfilling service requests, providing technical assistance, and ensuring effective communication throughout the support process. Overview The Service Desk is the first level of IT support. It receives requests from users through various communication channels such as phone calls, emails, chat, self-service portals, or ticketing systems. The primary goal of the Service Desk is to restore normal service as quickly as possible while maintaining high customer satisfaction. Scope The Service Desk handles: Incident logging Service request fulfillment Initial troubleshooting User communication Ticket prioritization Ticket escalation Ticket closure Knowledge sharing Responsibilities End User Report issues Provide required information Confirm issue resolution Service Desk Analyst Log tickets Categorize incidents Assign priorities Perform first-level troubleshooting Resolve common issues Escalate unresolved issues Update users on ticket progress Support Teams Resolve complex issues Provide technical expertise Update ticket status Service Desk Workflow User reports an issue or request. Service Desk logs the ticket. Ticket is categorized. Priority is assigned. Initial troubleshooting is performed. If resolved, ticket is closed. If unresolved, ticket is escalated. User confirms resolution. Ticket is closed. Common Activities Reset passwords Unlock user accounts Install software Troubleshoot Outlook Resolve printer issues Configure VPN Assist with hardware problems Provide basic network support Best Practices Record every interaction. Use clear and professional communication. Follow SLA guidelines. Update ticket status regularly. Document resolutions. Use the knowledge base before escalating. Related ITIL Practices Incident Management Service Request Management Knowledge Management Problem Management Keywords Service Desk Help Desk Support Ticket Incident Service Request SPOC IT Support

title: what_is_itil
content: What is ITIL? Purpose This document provides an introduction to ITIL (Information Technology Infrastructure Library) and explains its role in IT Service Management (ITSM). It serves as a foundation for understanding how IT teams deliver reliable, efficient, and business-focused IT services. Overview ITIL is a globally recognized framework of best practices for managing IT services. It helps organizations design, deliver, support, and continually improve IT services while aligning them with business objectives. Rather than focusing only on fixing technical problems, ITIL emphasizes delivering value to users through structured processes, clear roles, and continuous improvement. Objectives of ITIL The primary objectives of ITIL are to: Improve the quality of IT services. Deliver consistent and reliable support. Minimize service disruptions. Increase customer satisfaction. Standardize IT processes. Improve communication between IT teams and business users. Support continual service improvement. Benefits of ITIL Organizations implementing ITIL can achieve: Faster incident resolution. Better service availability. Improved productivity. Reduced downtime. Better risk management. Improved resource utilization. Higher user satisfaction. Better compliance with organizational policies. Key Principles ITIL promotes the following principles: Focus on delivering value. Start where you are. Progress iteratively with feedback. Collaborate and promote visibility. Think and work holistically. Keep processes simple and practical. Continuously optimize and automate where appropriate. What is IT Service Management (ITSM)? IT Service Management (ITSM) is the practice of planning, delivering, operating, and improving IT services to meet business needs. ITIL provides the framework and best practices used to implement effective ITSM. In simple terms: ITSM = Managing IT Services ITIL = Best Practices for Managing IT Services Common ITIL Practices Some of the most commonly used ITIL practices include: Service Desk Incident Management Problem Management Change Enablement Service Request Management Knowledge Management Configuration Management Monitoring and Event Management Release Management Deployment Management Service Level Management Information Security Management Example Scenario Employee: "My Outlook is not opening." Using ITIL: User contacts the Service Desk. An Incident ticket is created. The issue is prioritized. A support engineer investigates. The issue is resolved. The user confirms the solution. The ticket is closed. If the issue occurs repeatedly, a Problem record may be created to identify the root cause. Roles in ITIL End User Reports issues and requests IT services. Service Desk Acts as the single point of contact between users and IT support teams. Help Desk Engineer Diagnoses and resolves technical issues or escalates them when necessary. Incident Manager Coordinates the resolution of major incidents. Change Manager Reviews, approves, and manages changes to IT services. Problem Manager Investigates recurring incidents and identifies permanent solutions. Incident vs Service Request Incident: An unexpected interruption or reduction in the quality of an IT service. Examples: Printer offline Laptop not booting Outlook crashing Service Request: A formal request from a user for a standard IT service. Examples: New laptop Software installation VPN access Email account creation ITIL Lifecycle (Simplified) Although ITIL 4 focuses on the Service Value System, many organizations still think in terms of these activities: Plan Improve Engage Design and Transition Obtain or Build Deliver and Support These activities work together to deliver value through IT services. Best Practices Always log incidents and service requests. Follow defined processes and procedures. Maintain accurate documentation. Communicate clearly with users. Meet Service Level Agreement (SLA) targets. Keep knowledge articles up to date. Review recurring issues for long-term improvement. Continuously improve IT services based on feedback. Related Concepts ITSM Service Desk Incident Management Problem Management Change Enablement Knowledge Management Service Request Management Service Level Management Frequently Asked Questions Is ITIL software? No. ITIL is a framework of best practices. It is not a software application. Is ITIL mandatory? No. Organizations choose to adopt ITIL because it improves service management and operational efficiency. Can small organizations use ITIL? Yes. ITIL practices can be adapted for organizations of any size. Does ITIL only apply to IT departments? No. Many ITIL principles can also improve service management in other business functions. Summary ITIL provides a structured approach to managing IT services. It helps organizations improve service quality, reduce downtime, standardize support processes, and deliver greater value to users. By following ITIL best practices, IT teams can respond to incidents efficiently, fulfill service requests consistently, and continually improve their services. Keywords ITIL ITSM Service Management Incident Problem Change Service Desk Knowledge Base Help Desk SLA Service Request IT Support Best Practices

title: Email Access Request
content: Email Access Request Purpose Provide authorized users with access to their corporate email account. Scope Applicable to: New employees Existing employees requiring mailbox access Users changing departments Prerequisites Manager approval Valid employee account Microsoft 365 license available Procedure Step 1 Receive the service request. Step 2 Verify user identity. Step 3 Verify approvals. Step 4 Assign Microsoft 365 license. Step 5 Create or enable mailbox. Step 6 Configure Outlook. Step 7 Test email sending and receiving. Step 8 Share login instructions with the user. Common Issues License unavailable Mailbox synchronization delay Incorrect email configuration Authentication issues Escalation Escalate if: Mailbox cannot be created. Exchange service is unavailable. Licensing issues exist. Related ITIL Practices Service Request Management Access Management Keywords Email Mailbox Outlook Microsoft 365 Exchange License

title: New Laptop Request
content: New Laptop Request Purpose This document describes the standard process for requesting and issuing a new company laptop. Scope Applicable to: New employees Existing employees requiring replacement Employees with approved hardware upgrades Prerequisites Before processing the request: Manager approval obtained. Laptop available in inventory. User account created (if applicable). Asset record ready. Procedure Step 1 Receive the service request. Step 2 Verify management approval. Step 3 Check laptop availability in inventory. Step 4 Assign the laptop asset to the employee. Step 5 Install the standard operating system. Step 6 Install approved software. Examples: Microsoft Office VPN Client Antivirus Microsoft Teams Step 7 Configure user account. Step 8 Verify internet connectivity. Step 9 Hand over the laptop. Step 10 Update the Asset Management System. Common Issues Device unavailable Missing approvals Incorrect asset allocation Software installation failure Escalation Escalate if: Inventory is unavailable. Procurement is required. Hardware is defective. Related ITIL Practices Service Request Management Asset Management Keywords Laptop Asset Hardware Employee Request Inventory

title: Service Request Overview
content: Service Request Overview Purpose This document explains what a Service Request is and how it differs from an Incident. Definition A Service Request is a formal request from a user for a standard IT service, information, access, or equipment. Unlike incidents, service requests do not involve unexpected service interruptions. Examples New laptop Software installation VPN access Email account creation Shared folder access Printer installation Password change Monitor request Service Request Lifecycle User submits request. Service Desk reviews the request. Required approvals are obtained. Request is assigned. Request is fulfilled. User confirms completion. Request is closed. Difference Between Incident and Service Request Incident Unexpected issue requiring restoration of service. Example: Outlook crashes. Service Request User requests a standard service. Example: Install Microsoft Office. Best Practices Verify approvals before fulfillment. Follow standard procedures. Update users regularly. Close requests only after confirmation. Related ITIL Practices Service Request Management Service Desk Keywords Service Request Fulfillment Approval Access Software Hardware

title: Software Installation Request
content: Software Installation Request Purpose Provide a standard process for installing approved software on company-managed devices. Scope Applicable to all employees requesting authorized software. Prerequisites Approved request. User authorization. Administrator privileges. Valid software license. Procedure Step 1 Receive the software installation request. Step 2 Verify management approval if required. Step 3 Confirm the software is approved by the IT department. Step 4 Verify license availability. Step 5 Install the software. Step 6 Apply updates and patches. Step 7 Launch the application to verify successful installation. Step 8 Confirm with the user that the software is working correctly. Common Issues Insufficient permissions License unavailable Installation failure Compatibility issue Antivirus blocking installation Escalation Escalate if: Software requires vendor support. License procurement is required. Compatibility issues exist. Installation repeatedly fails. Best Practices Install only approved software. Keep software updated. Remove unused applications. Document software versions. Related ITIL Practices Service Request Management Change Enablement Asset Management Keywords Software Installation Application License Request Approval

title: Outlook Troubleshooting
content: Outlook Troubleshooting Purpose Resolve common Microsoft Outlook issues. Common Symptoms Outlook crashes Cannot send emails Cannot receive emails Slow Outlook Profile errors Troubleshooting Steps Restart Outlook. Restart the computer. Open Outlook in Safe Mode. Disable unnecessary add-ins. Repair Microsoft Office. Create a new Outlook profile. Verify internet connection. Escalation Escalate if: Exchange server is unavailable. Multiple users are affected. Mailbox corruption is suspected. Keywords Outlook Office Email Mailbox Microsoft 365

title: Printer Troubleshooting
content: Printer Troubleshooting Purpose Provide quick troubleshooting steps for common printer problems. Common Symptoms Printer offline Paper jam Print queue stuck Cannot print Poor print quality Troubleshooting Steps Ensure printer is powered on. Check network or USB cable. Restart the printer. Clear print queue. Restart Print Spooler service. Reinstall or update printer driver. Print a test page. Escalation Escalate if: Hardware fault exists. Printer cannot connect to network. Multiple users cannot print. Keywords Printer Offline Print Queue Driver Paper Jam

title: Slow Computer Troubleshooting
content: Slow Computer Troubleshooting Purpose This guide helps diagnose and resolve slow computer performance issues. Common Symptoms Slow startup Applications take a long time to open System freezes High CPU usage High memory usage Slow internet despite good connection Troubleshooting Steps Restart the computer. Close unnecessary applications. Check CPU and Memory usage using Task Manager. Disable unnecessary startup applications. Delete temporary files. Ensure at least 20% free disk space. Run Windows Update. Scan the system using antivirus software. Restart and verify performance. Common Causes Too many startup applications Low RAM Low disk space Malware Outdated Windows Background processes Escalation Escalate if: Hardware failure is suspected. SSD/HDD is failing. Performance remains poor after troubleshooting. Keywords Slow PC Performance CPU RAM Windows Disk

title: Microsoft Teams Troubleshooting
content: Microsoft Teams Troubleshooting Purpose Resolve common Microsoft Teams issues. Common Symptoms Teams not opening Login failure Audio not working Camera not working Poor call quality Troubleshooting Steps Restart Microsoft Teams. Sign out and sign in again. Check internet connectivity. Clear Teams cache. Update Teams. Verify microphone and camera permissions. Restart the computer. Escalation Escalate if: Microsoft Teams service is unavailable. Multiple users experience the same issue. Licensing issue exists. Keywords Teams Meeting Camera Microphone Microsoft 365

title: Wi-Fi Troubleshooting
content: Wi-Fi Troubleshooting Purpose Resolve wireless network connectivity issues. Common Symptoms Cannot connect to Wi-Fi Frequent disconnections Slow internet Limited connectivity Troubleshooting Steps Verify Wi-Fi is enabled. Restart Wi-Fi adapter. Restart the router if applicable. Forget and reconnect to the network. Run Windows Network Troubleshooter. Update Wi-Fi drivers. Test internet connectivity. Common Causes Weak signal Incorrect password Router issue Driver issue ISP outage Escalation Escalate if: Multiple users are affected. Wireless access point is down. Router requires replacement. Keywords Wi-Fi Wireless Internet Router Network
